question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 1 — Photograph Description]

[Photo description: A woman in a business suit is standing at a podium, speaking into a microphone. Behind her, a large screen displays a presentation slide with charts. Several people in the audience are taking notes.]

Look at the photo for question 1.

(A) The woman is writing on a whiteboard.
(B) The woman is giving a presentation at a podium.
(C) The audience is leaving the room.
(D) The screen behind her is blank.

questions.stem: What best describes the photo?
choices: (A) The woman is writing on a whiteboard.
(B) The woman is giving a presentation at a podium.
(C) The audience is leaving the room.
(D) The screen behind her is blank.
questions.explanation: 写真説明より「standing at a podium, speaking into a microphone ... presentation slide」→ (B) が正解。(A) ホワイトボードではない、(C) 聴衆はノートを取っている、(D) スライドが映っている。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 1 — Photograph Description]

[Photo description: Two construction workers wearing hard hats and safety vests are examining blueprints spread on a wooden table. Behind them is an unfinished building with scaffolding.]

Look at the photo for question 2.

(A) The workers are repairing the scaffolding.
(B) The blueprints are rolled up on the table.
(C) The workers are reviewing the blueprints.
(D) The building has been completed.

questions.stem: What best describes the photo?
choices: (A) The workers are repairing the scaffolding.
(B) The blueprints are rolled up on the table.
(C) The workers are reviewing the blueprints.
questions.explanation: 「examining blueprints spread on a wooden table」→ (C)。(A) 修理ではなく検討、(B) blueprintsは広げられている、(D) 建物は未完成。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 2 — Question and Response × 5]

Q1: Whose laptop is on the conference table?
  (A) It belongs to Mr. Chen from sales.
  (B) The conference starts at 2 PM.
  (C) Yes, I have a new laptop.

Q2: When will the renovated lobby be ready?
  (A) The lobby is on the ground floor.
  (B) Hopefully by the end of next month.
  (C) Renovations are very expensive.

Q3: Why are sales declining in our European market?
  (A) Currency fluctuations and increased competition.
  (B) Sales last year were record-high.
  (C) Europe has many countries.

Q4: Have you submitted the budget proposal yet?
  (A) I sent it to finance yesterday afternoon.
  (B) The budget was approved last quarter.
  (C) Yes, the proposal is on my desk.

Q5: Should we hire an external consultant or handle this internally?
  (A) The consultant arrived this morning.
  (B) I think internally would be more cost-effective.
  (C) External calls are forwarded to reception.

questions.stem: Q1の正答は?
choices: (A) It belongs to Mr. Chen from sales.
questions.explanation: Whose 質問 → 所有者の応答 (A)。

questions.stem: Q2の正答は?
choices: (B) Hopefully by the end of next month.
questions.explanation: When 質問 → 時期の応答 (B)。

questions.stem: Q3の正答は?
questions.explanation: Why 質問 → 原因の応答 (A)。

questions.stem: Q4の正答は?
choices: (A) I sent it to finance yesterday afternoon.
questions.explanation: Have you submitted → 進捗応答 (A)。

questions.stem: Q5の正答は?
choices: (B) I think internally would be more cost-effective.
questions.explanation: or 選択質問 → 意見応答 (B)。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 2 — Question and Response × 5]

Q1: How many copies of the report should I make?
  (A) The copy machine is broken.
  (B) Twelve, one for each board member.
  (C) Reports are due Friday.

Q2: Could you recommend a good restaurant for a business dinner?
  (A) The Italian place on 5th Avenue is excellent.
  (B) I had dinner already.
  (C) Business is going well.

Q3: What time does the presentation start tomorrow?
  (A) The auditorium can hold 200 people.
  (B) Sharp at 10 AM in the main auditorium.
  (C) I'll prepare my slides tonight.

Q4: Do you know if the new printer has been installed yet?
  (A) Yes, IT installed it this morning.
  (B) The old printer was very slow.
  (C) I print most documents at home.

Q5: Where should I leave the documents for Ms. Park?
  (A) Just put them on her desk in the corner office.
  (B) The documents are confidential.
  (C) Ms. Park is on vacation.

questions.stem: Q1の正答は?
choices: (B) Twelve, one for each board member.
questions.explanation: How many → 数の応答 (B)。

questions.stem: Q2の正答は?
choices: (A) The Italian place on 5th Avenue is excellent.
questions.explanation: Could you recommend → 推薦応答 (A)。

questions.stem: Q3の正答は?
choices: (B) Sharp at 10 AM in the main auditorium.
questions.explanation: What time → 時刻の応答 (B)。

questions.stem: Q4の正答は?
choices: (A) Yes, IT installed it this morning.
questions.explanation: Do you know if → Yes/No 応答 (A)。

questions.stem: Q5の正答は?
choices: (A) Just put them on her desk in the corner office.
questions.explanation: Where → 場所の応答 (A)。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 3 — Conversation, ~50 sec]

M: Hi Maria, I heard the marketing department is reorganizing. Have you been affected?
W: Yes, actually. They've moved me from product marketing to digital strategy. I start the new role on Monday.
M: Congratulations! That sounds like a promotion. Are you excited?
W: Definitely. The digital team has been driving most of our growth recently. It's where the company is investing heavily. The challenge will be learning new tools — analytics platforms I haven't used before.
M: Have they offered training?
W: They're sending me to a three-day conference next month, plus paying for an online certification course. So I should be well-prepared.

questions.stem: What change has happened to the woman?
choices: She was moved from product marketing to digital strategy
questions.explanation: 女性「They've moved me from product marketing to digital strategy」が根拠。

questions.stem: What concern does the woman mention?
choices: Learning new analytics tools she hasn't used
questions.explanation: 女性「The challenge will be learning new tools — analytics platforms I haven't used before」が根拠。

questions.stem: What training has the company offered?
choices: A three-day conference + online certification course
questions.explanation: 女性「sending me to a three-day conference next month, plus paying for an online certification course」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 3 — Conversation, ~50 sec]

W: Tom, the IT department is asking everyone to update their passwords this week. Have you done yours yet?
M: Not yet, Jennifer. Honestly, I find the password requirements really frustrating — 14 characters, uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special symbols. I can never remember them.
W: I know what you mean. But there have been three security incidents in our industry this year, so I understand why they're being strict. Have you tried using a password manager?
M: I've heard of them but never used one. Do you recommend any?
W: I use one called LockSafe. It generates strong passwords automatically and remembers them for you. The basic version is free.
M: That sounds useful. Could you send me the link?

questions.stem: What is the IT department asking employees to do?
questions.explanation: 女性「IT department is asking everyone to update their passwords this week」が根拠。

questions.stem: Why does the woman think strict requirements are necessary?
choices: Three security incidents in their industry this year
questions.explanation: 女性「there have been three security incidents in our industry this year, so I understand why they're being strict」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does the woman suggest?
choices: Using a password manager called LockSafe (free basic version)
questions.explanation: 女性「I use one called LockSafe ... The basic version is free」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 4 — Talk / Announcement, ~45 sec]

Good afternoon, ladies and gentlemen. This is your captain speaking. Welcome aboard Pacific Airlines flight 872 with non-stop service to Singapore. Our flight time today will be approximately 7 hours and 15 minutes, and we'll be cruising at an altitude of 35,000 feet.

The weather in Singapore is clear and warm — currently 28 degrees Celsius. We expect a smooth flight with only light turbulence over the South China Sea about three hours into the flight.

Our cabin crew will begin meal service shortly after takeoff. We have three options today: chicken with rice, beef pasta, and a vegetarian option of vegetable curry. Please let your flight attendant know your preference.

For those of you needing to connect to other flights upon arrival, please check the screens in the arrival hall for your gate information. We expect to land on time at approximately 6:30 PM local time.

Sit back, relax, and enjoy your flight.

questions.stem: Who is the speaker?
questions.explanation: 冒頭「This is your captain speaking」が根拠。

questions.stem: How long is the flight?
questions.explanation: 「Our flight time today will be approximately 7 hours and 15 minutes」が根拠。

questions.stem: What meal options are available?
choices: Chicken with rice, beef pasta, vegetarian curry
questions.explanation: 「three options today: chicken with rice, beef pasta, and a vegetarian option of vegetable curry」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[TOEIC Listening Part 4 — Talk / Voice Mail, ~45 sec]

Hi, this is Robert calling from Westside Plumbing Services. I'm calling about your appointment scheduled for tomorrow, Tuesday, at 10 AM to repair the kitchen sink leak. Unfortunately, our plumber Steve who was assigned to your job has called in sick this morning.

We have two options for you. First, we can reschedule with another plumber, Mark, who is available the same day at 2 PM. Second, if you'd prefer Steve specifically, we can move your appointment to Thursday at 9 AM.

Please call me back at 555-3389 to let me know which option works for you. If I don't hear from you by 5 PM today, I'll assume you'd like the Thursday appointment with Steve.

Again, that's 555-3389. Thank you, and we apologize for the inconvenience.

questions.stem: Why is the speaker calling?
choices: To inform that the assigned plumber called in sick and offer rescheduling options
questions.explanation: 「our plumber Steve who was assigned to your job has called in sick」 + 2 options を提示。

questions.stem: What is the deadline to call back?
questions.explanation: 「If I don't hear from you by 5 PM today」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the default option if no callback?
questions.explanation: 「I'll assume you'd like the Thursday appointment with Steve」が根拠。

questions.stem: The marketing campaign was ( ) successful that the company exceeded its sales target by 40%.
questions.explanation: so + 形容詞 + that 構文。such + 名詞 + that。

questions.stem: All visitors must ( ) at the front desk before entering the office area.
questions.explanation: must + 動詞原形 → register。

questions.stem: The merger ( ) by the end of next quarter, pending regulatory approval.
questions.explanation: by the end of next quarter (未来) + 受動態 → will be completed。

questions.stem: Customer feedback indicates that response times have improved ( ) over the last six months.
questions.explanation: improved (動詞) を修飾 → 副詞 significantly。

questions.stem: The board members were ( ) impressed by the new product prototype.
questions.explanation: impressed (過去分詞) を修飾 → 副詞 thoroughly。

questions.stem: The new training program is designed to ( ) employees with the latest industry practices.
questions.explanation: to + 動詞原形 → familiarize (動詞: 〜に精通させる)。

questions.stem: Profits in the third quarter were ( ) higher than those in the second quarter.
questions.explanation: higher (比較級) を修飾 → 副詞 considerably。

questions.stem: Despite the challenging market conditions, the team ( ) all sales targets last year.
questions.explanation: last year (過去) → 過去形 met。

questions.stem: The renovation project is expected to be completed ( ) the end of December.
questions.explanation: completion deadline → by (期限まで)。

questions.stem: All new employees receive a comprehensive ( ) on company policies during their first week.
questions.explanation: a comprehensive + 名詞 → orientation。

question_data.passage:
To: All Staff
From: Building Management
Subject: Parking Lot Resurfacing

Dear Tenants,

We are writing to inform you that the main parking lot will be (1) ( ) for resurfacing from June 10th through June 14th. During this five-day period, no parking will be available in the main lot.

For your convenience, we have arranged (2) ( ) parking at the lot located one block north on Maple Street. Shuttle service will run every 15 minutes between this temporary lot and our building from 7 AM to 7 PM.

We (3) ( ) for any inconvenience this may cause and appreciate your patience as we improve our facilities.

Sincerely,
Building Management

questions.explanation: will be + 過去分詞 (受動態未来) → closed。
alternative parking = 代替の駐車場 (形容詞)。alternate も可だが文脈的には alternative が一般的。
主語 We + 現在形動詞 → apologize。

question_data.passage:
Dear Mr. Anderson,

Thank you for your interest in our company's executive coaching program. We were (1) ( ) to receive your application and learn about your professional goals.

After (2) ( ) your materials, our team has matched you with Coach Patricia Kim, who specializes in leadership development for technology executives. Coach Kim has over 20 years of experience and has worked with senior leaders at major firms.

Your first session is scheduled for Tuesday, March 15th at 2 PM (3) ( ) virtual platform Zoom. Coach Kim will send you a meeting link the day before your session.

Welcome to the program.

Best regards,
Elite Coaching Services

questions.explanation: were + 過去分詞 → pleased。
After + 動名詞 → reviewing。
via + プラットフォーム = 〜経由で。

question_data.passage:
GreenLeaf Café — Now Hiring

We're a growing organic café in downtown Riverside, looking for friendly, motivated team members.

Positions Available:
• Baristas (Full-time and Part-time)
• Sous Chef (Full-time)
• Kitchen Assistants (Part-time)
• Front-of-House Manager (Full-time)

What We Offer:
- Competitive hourly wages starting at $18/hour for entry-level positions
- Free meals during shifts
- Health benefits for all full-time employees after 60 days
- Flexible scheduling for part-time staff (great for students)
- Career growth opportunities — many of our managers started as baristas!

Ideal candidates have:
• Excellent customer service skills
• Ability to work in a fast-paced environment
• Passion for organic, sustainable food
• Previous food service experience preferred but not required for some positions

How to Apply:
Send your resume and a brief cover letter to careers@greenleafcafe.com. Mention which position interests you. Walk-in interviews are welcome on weekdays between 2-4 PM.

GreenLeaf Café is an equal opportunity employer.

questions.stem: What is the starting hourly wage for entry-level positions?
questions.explanation: 「Competitive hourly wages starting at $18/hour for entry-level positions」が根拠。

questions.stem: After how long do full-time employees get health benefits?
questions.explanation: 「Health benefits for all full-time employees after 60 days」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is special about the company's career path?
choices: Many managers started as baristas — internal advancement
questions.explanation: 「Career growth opportunities — many of our managers started as baristas」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
=== Document 1: Email ===

From: tanaka@globalsupply.com
To: orders@stationerymax.com
Subject: Bulk Order Inquiry
Date: April 14

Hello,

We are a corporate office of 250 employees and would like to place a large order for office supplies. We need:
- 1,500 ballpoint pens (black ink)
- 50 boxes of A4 paper (5,000 sheets each)
- 100 binders (assorted colors)
- 30 staplers (heavy-duty)

Could you provide a quote with bulk discount? We would also appreciate information about delivery options for an order of this size, as our office is in downtown Tokyo.

Thanks,
Kaori Tanaka
Procurement Manager, Global Supply Co.

=== Document 2: Reply Email ===

From: orders@stationerymax.com
To: tanaka@globalsupply.com
Subject: Re: Bulk Order Inquiry
Date: April 15

Dear Ms. Tanaka,

Thank you for your inquiry. We're pleased to offer the following pricing for your order:

- Pens: $0.45/unit ($675 for 1,500) — bulk discount applied (regular $0.60/unit)
- A4 paper boxes: $35/box ($1,750 for 50 boxes) — 30% bulk discount
- Binders: $4.20/unit ($420 for 100)
- Heavy-duty staplers: $28/unit ($840 for 30)

Subtotal: $3,685
Delivery to downtown Tokyo: Free for orders over $3,000
Grand Total: $3,685

Delivery would be approximately 5 business days from order confirmation. We can split delivery into two shipments at no additional charge if you prefer.

Please confirm by April 20th to lock in these prices.

Best regards,
David Park
StationeryMax Sales Team

questions.stem: How many employees does Global Supply Co. have?
questions.explanation: Document 1「a corporate office of 250 employees」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the total cost of the order?
questions.explanation: Document 2「Grand Total: $3,685」が根拠。

questions.stem: Why is delivery free?
questions.explanation: Document 2「Delivery to downtown Tokyo: Free for orders over $3,000」が根拠。Subtotal $3,685 が $3,000 超えなので無料。

questions.stem: By when must Ms. Tanaka confirm to lock in prices?
questions.explanation: Document 2「Please confirm by April 20th to lock in these prices」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[IELTS Listening Section 1 - Apartment Rental Inquiry]

Agent: Good morning, Riverside Properties, this is Sarah speaking.

Caller: Hi, I'm calling about the apartment listing on Park Avenue.

Agent: Yes, the two-bedroom one? It's still available.

Caller: Could you tell me more about it?

Agent: Sure. It's on the 4th floor of a modern building. The total area is 75 square meters. There are two bedrooms, one bathroom, a kitchen, and a living room with a balcony overlooking the river.

Caller: How much is the monthly rent?

Agent: It's £1,800 per month, which includes water and basic internet. Electricity and heating are separate.

Caller: Is parking available?

Agent: Yes, an underground parking space costs an additional £80 per month.

Caller: What about the lease terms?

Agent: Minimum 12 months, with the option to renew. We require a deposit equal to 6 weeks' rent.

Caller: When is the apartment available?

Agent: From August 1st. Would you like to schedule a viewing?

Caller: Yes, please. How about Saturday at 11 AM?

Agent: That works. Could I have your name, please?

Caller: It's Michael Brennan. B-R-E-N-N-A-N.

Agent: Thank you, Mr. Brennan. I'll see you Saturday at 11 AM.

questions.stem: What is the size of the apartment?
questions.explanation: 「The total area is 75 square meters」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is included in the monthly rent?
questions.explanation: 「£1,800 per month, which includes water and basic internet」が根拠。

questions.stem: How much extra is parking?
questions.explanation: 「underground parking space costs an additional £80 per month」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the minimum lease term?
questions.explanation: 「Minimum 12 months, with the option to renew」が根拠。

questions.stem: When is the viewing scheduled?
questions.explanation: 「How about Saturday at 11 AM? ... That works」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[IELTS Listening Section 2 - Public Talk: Community Garden Tour]

Welcome, everyone, to the Eastside Community Garden. My name is Linda Chen, and I'll be your guide today. Our garden has been operating for over 15 years and has become a vital part of the neighborhood.

Let me give you an overview of what you'll see today. The garden covers about two acres and is divided into four main sections.

The first section, immediately to your left, is the Community Plot Area, where 60 individual families rent plots to grow their own vegetables and flowers. Each plot is about 4 square meters, and the annual rental fee is just £30.

Moving forward, the central section is our Communal Growing Area. Volunteers maintain this space, and the produce is donated to local food banks. Last year alone, we donated over 800 kilograms of fresh vegetables to families in need.

To your right, you'll see the Education Garden. We host school visits and after-school programs here. Children learn about plants, sustainability, and where their food comes from. Approximately 1,200 children visit each year.

At the far end of the garden is the Sensory Garden, designed specifically for elderly visitors and people with disabilities. It features fragrant herbs, textured plants, and accessible raised beds. This is our newest addition, opened just last spring.

We rely entirely on volunteers and community donations. If you're interested in getting involved, please pick up a brochure at the entrance, or speak to me after the tour.

questions.stem: How long has the garden been operating?
questions.explanation: 「Our garden has been operating for over 15 years」が根拠。

questions.stem: How much vegetables were donated to food banks last year?
questions.explanation: 「Last year alone, we donated over 800 kilograms of fresh vegetables」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the newest addition to the garden?
questions.explanation: 「This is our newest addition, opened just last spring」が Sensory Garden の説明部分に該当。

questions.stem: Who is the Sensory Garden designed for?
choices: Elderly visitors and people with disabilities
questions.explanation: 「designed specifically for elderly visitors and people with disabilities」が根拠。

questions.stem: How is the garden funded?
questions.explanation: 「We rely entirely on volunteers and community donations」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[IELTS Listening Section 3 - Tutorial Discussion: Research Project on Sleep]

Tutor: Good morning, Sophia and Daniel. Let's discuss your research project on sleep patterns among university students. Where are you with the literature review?

Sophia: We've reviewed 45 papers so far, focusing on three main areas — sleep duration, sleep quality indicators, and the impact of digital device use on sleep.

Daniel: We're particularly interested in how smartphone use before bed affects sleep onset and quality. There's a lot of recent research on this.

Tutor: That's a relevant focus. Have you decided on your research methodology?

Sophia: We're planning a mixed-methods approach — a survey of 300 students plus in-depth interviews with 15 students.

Daniel: The survey will collect quantitative data on sleep habits, while interviews will explore qualitative aspects — like why students choose to use devices despite knowing the effects.

Tutor: Have you considered using sleep tracking apps to gather objective data?

Sophia: We discussed it but decided against it. The technology would add complexity, and self-reported data is typical in this field of research.

Tutor: I understand the choice, but it might be worth mentioning the limitation in your discussion. Without objective measurements, you're relying on what students report — which can differ from actual behavior.

Daniel: Good point. We'll add that as a methodology limitation.

Tutor: One more thing — have you obtained ethics approval?

Sophia: The application is submitted. We expect approval by next Monday.

questions.stem: How many papers have they reviewed?
questions.explanation: Sophia「reviewed 45 papers so far」が根拠。

questions.stem: What methodology are they using?
choices: Mixed methods - survey of 300 + interviews with 15
questions.explanation: Sophia「a survey of 300 students plus in-depth interviews with 15 students」が根拠。

questions.stem: Why did they decide against sleep tracking apps?
questions.explanation: Sophia「The technology would add complexity, and self-reported data is typical in this field」が根拠。

questions.stem: What limitation does the tutor suggest mentioning?
choices: Reliance on self-reported data which can differ from actual behavior
questions.explanation: Tutor「relying on what students report — which can differ from actual behavior」が根拠。

questions.stem: When do they expect ethics approval?
questions.explanation: Sophia「expect approval by next Monday」が根拠。

question_data.audio_script:
[IELTS Listening Section 4 - Academic Lecture: The History of Coffee]

Good morning, everyone. Today's lecture explores the global journey of coffee — from a small Ethiopian plant to the world's most traded agricultural commodity.

Coffee originated in the highlands of Ethiopia, where wild coffee plants grew. According to legend, a goat herder named Kaldi discovered coffee around the 9th century when he noticed his goats becoming energetic after eating berries from a particular bush.

From Ethiopia, coffee cultivation spread to Yemen by the 15th century, where it became the first cultivated coffee region outside Africa. The port city of Mocha gave its name to the famous coffee variety we still know today.

Coffee reached Europe in the 17th century, primarily through Venetian traders. Initially viewed with suspicion as a 'Muslim drink,' coffee gained acceptance after Pope Clement VIII reportedly tasted it and gave his blessing. The first European coffee houses opened in Venice around 1645, followed by establishments in Vienna, Paris, and London within decades.

The colonial period dramatically expanded coffee cultivation. Dutch traders introduced coffee to Java in the late 1600s, French colonists brought it to Caribbean territories, and Portuguese settlers established massive plantations in Brazil. By the early 19th century, Brazil had become the world's largest coffee producer — a position it still holds today.

The 20th century saw the industrialization of coffee production. Instant coffee was developed in 1901 by a Japanese-American chemist. Vacuum packaging, invented in the 1930s, allowed coffee to be transported globally without losing freshness.

Today, coffee is grown commercially in over 70 countries, employs 25 million farmers, and is consumed at a rate of 2.25 billion cups per day worldwide. The industry faces new challenges — climate change threatens traditional growing regions, while consumer preferences for ethically-sourced and specialty coffee are reshaping markets.

questions.stem: Where did coffee originate?
questions.explanation: 「Coffee originated in the highlands of Ethiopia」が根拠。

questions.stem: When did the first European coffee houses open?
questions.explanation: 「The first European coffee houses opened in Venice around 1645」が根拠。

questions.stem: When did instant coffee get developed?
questions.explanation: 「Instant coffee was developed in 1901 by a Japanese-American chemist」が根拠。

questions.stem: How many farmers does coffee currently employ?
questions.explanation: 「employs 25 million farmers」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is one challenge facing the coffee industry today?
choices: Climate change threatening traditional growing regions
questions.explanation: 「climate change threatens traditional growing regions」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Hummingbirds: Tiny Marvels of Evolution

Hummingbirds are among the most remarkable birds on Earth. Despite weighing only between two and twenty grams, they perform feats of flight unmatched by any other birds. They can hover in mid-air, fly backwards, and even fly upside down for brief periods. The bee hummingbird, the world's smallest bird at just two grams, beats its wings approximately 80 times per second — too fast for human eyes to track.

What makes hummingbird flight unique is the structure of their shoulders and wings. Unlike most birds, whose shoulder joints allow movement primarily up and down, hummingbird shoulders rotate in a figure-eight pattern. This allows them to generate lift on both the upstroke and downstroke, enabling sustained hovering. Their wing muscles, which constitute up to 30% of their total body weight, contract incredibly rapidly to produce these complex motions.

This extraordinary flight ability comes at a metabolic cost. Hummingbirds have the highest mass-specific metabolic rate of any vertebrate. Their hearts beat over 1,200 times per minute during active flight. They must consume approximately half their body weight in nectar each day to fuel this metabolism, visiting hundreds or even thousands of flowers daily.

Despite their dependence on rapid energy intake, hummingbirds have a remarkable adaptation for survival during food scarcity or cold weather. They enter a state called torpor, in which their metabolic rate drops dramatically — heart rate falling to as low as 50 beats per minute, and body temperature dropping by up to 30 degrees Celsius. This allows them to conserve energy through nights and difficult periods that would otherwise be lethal.

Hummingbirds play crucial ecological roles as pollinators. As they feed on nectar, they transfer pollen between flowers, enabling reproduction in over 8,000 plant species. Some plants have co-evolved with specific hummingbird species, developing flower shapes, colors, and bloom times perfectly matched to their pollinator partners. The relationship is symbiotic — the plants depend on hummingbirds for reproduction, while hummingbirds depend on the nectar these plants produce.

Sadly, many hummingbird species face threats from habitat loss, climate change, and pesticide use. Of the approximately 360 hummingbird species, dozens are now considered endangered or threatened. Conservation efforts focus on protecting native habitats, planting nectar-rich gardens, and reducing pesticide use. The fate of these tiny birds is closely tied to the broader health of the ecosystems in which they evolved.

questions.stem: How fast does the bee hummingbird beat its wings?
questions.explanation: 段落1「beats its wings approximately 80 times per second」が根拠。

questions.stem: What unique feature enables hummingbird flight?
choices: Shoulders rotating in a figure-eight pattern
questions.explanation: 段落2「hummingbird shoulders rotate in a figure-eight pattern. This allows them to generate lift on both the upstroke and downstroke」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is torpor?
choices: State where metabolic rate drops dramatically; heart rate as low as 50 BPM, body temperature drops 30°C
questions.explanation: 段落4「torpor, in which their metabolic rate drops dramatically — heart rate falling to as low as 50 beats per minute, and body temperature dropping by up to 30 degrees Celsius」が根拠。

questions.stem: How many plant species depend on hummingbird pollination?
questions.explanation: 段落5「enabling reproduction in over 8,000 plant species」が根拠。

questions.stem: What threats do hummingbirds face?
choices: Habitat loss, climate change, pesticide use; dozens of species endangered
questions.explanation: 段落6「threats from habitat loss, climate change, and pesticide use ... dozens are now considered endangered」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Economics of Renewable Energy Transition

The transition from fossil fuels to renewable energy sources represents one of the most consequential economic transformations in history. Understanding the economics of this transition requires looking beyond simple price comparisons to consider the full range of costs and benefits involved.

The headline economics of renewable energy have shifted dramatically over the past decade. The cost of solar photovoltaic electricity has fallen by approximately 90% since 2010. Onshore wind has become the cheapest source of new electricity generation in many regions. Battery storage costs have similarly plummeted, addressing the intermittency challenges that long limited renewable adoption. In purely cost-of-generation terms, renewables now compete favorably with — and increasingly outcompete — fossil fuel alternatives.

However, the transition involves substantial transition costs that headline numbers obscure. Existing power plants represent enormous sunk investments. Decommissioning fossil fuel infrastructure before its economic lifespan ends imposes costs on the companies and workers who depend on it. Building new renewable infrastructure requires upfront capital expenditure that, while delivering long-term savings, must be financed during the transition period.

The distribution of these costs and benefits raises significant equity concerns. Communities economically dependent on fossil fuel industries face job losses and tax base erosion. Lower-income consumers may struggle with upfront costs of energy efficiency improvements or electric vehicles, even when long-term savings would benefit them. Developing nations question why they should bear transition costs for problems caused by historical emissions from wealthy countries. These distributional issues, more than the underlying technical economics, often shape political resistance to faster transition.

Government policy plays a crucial role in shaping how these costs and benefits are distributed. Carbon pricing mechanisms internalize the environmental costs of fossil fuels into market prices. Subsidies for renewable installation accelerate adoption. Investments in workforce transition help affected communities adapt to new economic realities. Trade policies determine whether the benefits of low-cost renewable manufacturing flow to consumers or to particular national champions.

Looking forward, the economics of the transition appear increasingly favorable as costs continue to decline and climate damages from continued fossil fuel use accumulate. The International Energy Agency projects that renewables will supply over 80% of new electricity generation through 2030. Continuing technological progress, particularly in storage and grid integration, suggests this trajectory may accelerate further. Yet realizing these economic possibilities depends on policy choices, infrastructure investments, and successful navigation of transition challenges that pure economic analysis cannot fully predict.

The ultimate question is not whether renewable transition makes economic sense, but how to manage the substantial economic transformation it requires.

questions.stem: How much has the cost of solar photovoltaic electricity fallen since 2010?
questions.explanation: 段落2「The cost of solar photovoltaic electricity has fallen by approximately 90% since 2010」が根拠。

questions.stem: What 'transition costs' does the author identify?
choices: Sunk investments in existing plants, decommissioning costs, upfront capital for new infrastructure
questions.explanation: 段落3「Existing power plants represent enormous sunk investments. Decommissioning fossil fuel infrastructure ... imposes costs ... upfront capital expenditure」が根拠。

questions.stem: What equity concerns does the author raise?
choices: Job/tax base erosion in fossil-fuel communities, upfront costs for low-income consumers, developing nations bearing historical emission costs
questions.explanation: 段落4「Communities economically dependent on fossil fuel industries face job losses ... Lower-income consumers may struggle with upfront costs ... Developing nations question why they should bear transition costs」が根拠。

questions.stem: What role does the author say government policy plays?
choices: Crucial — shaping cost/benefit distribution through carbon pricing, subsidies, workforce transition, trade policies
questions.explanation: 段落5「Government policy plays a crucial role in shaping how these costs and benefits are distributed」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the IEA projection?
choices: Renewables will supply over 80% of new electricity generation through 2030
questions.explanation: 段落6「International Energy Agency projects that renewables will supply over 80% of new electricity generation through 2030」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Cognitive Revolution Reconsidered

The so-called cognitive revolution that transformed psychology in the mid-twentieth century is one of the most influential intellectual movements in modern science. Yet historians of science increasingly recognize that the standard narrative of the revolution oversimplifies a complex process and obscures important continuities with earlier traditions.

The standard narrative goes roughly as follows. From the early twentieth century until the 1950s, behaviorism dominated American psychology. Behaviorists, following B.F. Skinner and his predecessors, focused exclusively on observable behavior and rejected speculation about internal mental processes as unscientific. Then, in the 1950s and 1960s, a coalition of researchers — including George Miller, Noam Chomsky, Jerome Bruner, and Herbert Simon — challenged this paradigm. They argued that mental processes could be studied scientifically, drew analogies between minds and computers, and developed methods for investigating internal cognitive operations. By the 1970s, cognitive psychology had largely replaced behaviorism as the mainstream paradigm.

This narrative captures real developments but oversimplifies in several ways. First, behaviorism was never as monolithic or as hostile to mental processes as later accounts suggest. Edward Tolman's 'purposive behaviorism,' for example, made extensive use of internal cognitive concepts decades before the cognitive revolution was supposed to have begun. Karl Lashley, while methodologically aligned with behaviorism, conducted research on memory and brain function that anticipated later cognitive themes.

Second, cognitive psychology did not emerge solely from rebellion against behaviorism. European psychology, less dominated by behaviorism than its American counterpart, had developed sophisticated cognitive traditions. Gestalt psychology, the work of Jean Piaget on child development, and Soviet psychological research on memory and thought all influenced what eventually became American cognitive psychology. The cognitive revolution drew on these existing traditions as much as it developed new ideas.

Third, the role of computers in the cognitive revolution is often overstated. While computer metaphors did inspire cognitive theorizing, much of the empirical work of cognitive psychology — studies of memory, attention, perception, and reasoning — built on experimental methods predating computer technology. The computer was as much a marketing device as a theoretical foundation, providing legitimacy in an era enthralled by computational possibilities.

Reconsidering the cognitive revolution this way has practical implications for how we understand current developments in psychology and cognitive science. The current rise of computational neuroscience, machine learning approaches to cognition, and neuroscientific methods is sometimes presented as a second cognitive revolution. But this presentation may again oversimplify, missing continuities with cognitive psychology and obscuring the practical reasons for current methodological shifts.

The broader point is that scientific change rarely follows the dramatic narratives we construct around it. Important transitions involve gradual evolution, multiple parallel developments, and continuities alongside genuine innovations. Recognizing this complexity does not diminish the achievements of cognitive psychology — but it places those achievements in a more accurate historical context.

questions.stem: Who are some figures associated with the cognitive revolution?
choices: George Miller, Noam Chomsky, Jerome Bruner, Herbert Simon
questions.explanation: 段落2「George Miller, Noam Chomsky, Jerome Bruner, and Herbert Simon — challenged this paradigm」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is one way the standard narrative oversimplifies?
choices: Behaviorism was not as monolithic or hostile to mental processes as later accounts suggest (Tolman's purposive behaviorism)
questions.explanation: 段落3「behaviorism was never as monolithic or as hostile to mental processes as later accounts suggest. Edward Tolman's 'purposive behaviorism' ... extensive use of internal cognitive concepts」が根拠。

questions.stem: What European traditions influenced cognitive psychology?
choices: Gestalt psychology, Piaget's child development work, Soviet research on memory and thought
questions.explanation: 段落4「Gestalt psychology, the work of Jean Piaget on child development, and Soviet psychological research on memory and thought」が根拠。

questions.stem: How does the author characterize the role of computers?
choices: Often overstated; computer was as much a marketing device as theoretical foundation
questions.explanation: 段落5「The role of computers in the cognitive revolution is often overstated ... The computer was as much a marketing device as a theoretical foundation」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the broader point the author makes?
choices: Scientific change rarely follows dramatic narratives — involves gradual evolution, parallel developments, and continuities
questions.explanation: 段落7「scientific change rarely follows the dramatic narratives we construct around it. Important transitions involve gradual evolution, multiple parallel developments, and continuities」が根拠。

question_data.prompt:
[IELTS Writing Task 1 — 150 words minimum, 20 min]

The table below shows the percentage of households owning various electronic devices in three countries (Japan, Germany, Brazil) in 2010 and 2024.

| Device | Japan 2010 | Japan 2024 | Germany 2010 | Germany 2024 | Brazil 2010 | Brazil 2024 |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|------------|------------|
| Smartphone | 25% | 95% | 30% | 92% | 12% | 78% |
| Smart TV | 5% | 70% | 8% | 75% | 2% | 45% |
| Smart Speaker | 0% | 38% | 0% | 42% | 0% | 12% |
| Laptop Computer | 65% | 75% | 58% | 78% | 22% | 55% |

Summarize the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
question_data.sample:
The table illustrates the percentage of households owning four electronic devices — smartphones, smart TVs, smart speakers, and laptop computers — in Japan, Germany, and Brazil in 2010 compared to 2024.

Overall, all three countries experienced substantial growth in device ownership across all categories during this period, with smartphones showing the most dramatic increases.

Smartphone ownership rose remarkably in all three countries. Japan and Germany saw similar trajectories, increasing from 25% and 30% in 2010 to 95% and 92% respectively in 2024. Brazil showed the most striking growth, climbing from just 12% to 78%.

Smart TV adoption followed a similar pattern but at lower absolute levels. Germany led in 2024 at 75%, closely followed by Japan at 70%. Brazil reached 45%, up from a mere 2% in 2010.

Smart speakers, virtually nonexistent in 2010, became common in Japan (38%) and Germany (42%) by 2024, but remained relatively low in Brazil (12%).

Laptop ownership grew more modestly. Germany rose from 58% to 78%, Japan from 65% to 75%, and Brazil from 22% to 55% — the largest relative increase of the three. (197 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — IELTS Task 1 essay (150+ words, 20 min)
questions.explanation: 【IELTS Task 1】① Para1: 全体導入 (テーブル概要) ② Para2: Overall trend ③ Para3-5: 各 device の変化と国比較 ④ 数値の正確な引用 ⑤ 比較表現 (substantial / dramatic / striking / modest)。

question_data.prompt:
[IELTS Writing Task 2 — 250 words minimum, 40 min]

In many countries, the proportion of older people is steadily increasing. Some people see this as a positive development, while others view it as a serious problem.

Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
question_data.sample:
The aging of populations in many countries presents both opportunities and challenges that shape contemporary policy debates. After examining both perspectives, I believe the challenges are more significant but the opportunities deserve serious recognition.

Those who view population aging positively highlight several genuine benefits. Older citizens contribute valuable wisdom, mentorship, and stability to communities. Many older adults remain economically active well past traditional retirement age, providing experience and continuity that younger workers cannot match. Aging societies often show declining crime rates, more measured political discourse, and greater long-term thinking. Furthermore, longer healthy lifespans represent one of the great achievements of modern medicine and improved living standards.

However, the challenges are substantial and demand serious attention. Pension and healthcare systems designed for shorter lifespans face fundamental sustainability questions. The shrinking ratio of working-age citizens to retirees strains tax bases needed to fund social services. Healthcare expenditures rise dramatically as populations age, with chronic conditions requiring expensive long-term management. Labor shortages in physically demanding industries create economic pressures, while informal care responsibilities — typically falling on family members — strain household economies. Some nations face genuinely concerning demographic trajectories where current arrangements appear unsustainable without major reform.

In my view, while the achievements that produced longer lifespans deserve celebration, the systemic challenges require urgent policy attention. Pension systems must be reformed to reflect demographic realities. Healthcare systems need restructuring to address chronic conditions cost-effectively. Immigration policies, female labor force participation, and productivity improvements all need to be considered as partial responses. The aging trend is itself a positive achievement; the failure to adapt institutions to this new reality would be a serious problem. (271 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — IELTS Task 2 essay (250+ words, 40 min)
questions.explanation: 【IELTS Task 2】Discuss both views + own opinion 形式。Para1: 立場明示 introduction。Para2: positive 側論理。Para3: challenges 側論理。Para4: 自分の意見と policy 提案。271語、IELTS Band 7-8 水準。
